executive_summary: Sunday deliveries were strong — 13 orders worth $916.76 ex. HST (+51.9% vs 7d avg). Placed orders held steady at 12. But only 1 new customer showed up, so the day's ad spend ($106.84) bought just one acquisition, and the 7-day cost per new customer sits at $42.34 — well over the $25 ceiling.
analyst_brief:
Yesterday was a good financial day carried almost entirely by people who already know us. We delivered 13 orders worth $916.76 ex. HST — over half again the 7-day average — and 9 of the 12 placed orders (75%) came from regulars. Second-order and repeat customers held up nicely. The order size dipped a bit ($54.40 vs $60.82 average), but that's normal weekend variance and still comfortably above the $45 target. Ratings were excellent (4.86, no low scores). If you only looked at revenue, this was a strong Sunday.

The problem is at the front of the pipeline. Only **1 new customer** showed up. We spent $106.84 on ads to get that one person, so yesterday's cost per new customer was $106.84 — more than four times the $25 ceiling. One quiet day like that doesn't mean much on its own, which is why the honest number to watch is the 7-day rolling figure: **$42.34 per new customer** (17 new customers for $719.82 over the week). That has been sitting above $25 for a while now. Spend is steady (~$103/day) but it's converting fewer strangers into first orders. This is the one thing I'd keep my eye on — the business is running on its regulars, and the tank of new customers we refill each week is getting more expensive.

Two smaller things worth noting.

Cancellations are creeping up. The rolling 7-day cancel rate is 9.3%, up 2.1 points from the prior day's reading, and nearly double the steadier 30-day baseline of 4.8% (8 cancels this week vs 17 across the whole month). Not an emergency, but if it keeps climbing it's eating into orders we already paid to acquire. Worth a glance at what those 8 cancelled orders had in common.

Ad money is leaking into generic searches. A chunk of yesterday's spend went to broad "laundromat near me" / "laundry near me" clicks that produced zero orders (roughly $20+ across those terms). I'm flagging this as an observation only — do not add negative keywords without reviewing manually, since past broad negatives blocked good searches. Just something for a human to eyeball.

On SEO: all 42 monitored pages are indexed, nothing broken. The Hamilton city page got 399 impressions and **zero clicks** (ranking ~12.6, just off page 1), and Toronto is stuck at position 21.6 despite 417 impressions. Google is showing these pages but nobody's clicking. Since the city pages share one template for their title and description, a rewrite there would lift all 24 at once — a higher-leverage 20-minute job than tweaking a single page.
validation_notes: DB consistent: 12 placed × $54.40 ≈ $652.77; 13 delivered × $70.52 ≈ $916.76 (net of 13% HST, no tips). Google Ads and GSC pulled OK. Stripe, GA4, Intercom, ClickUp unavailable — no independent payment cross-check today.
data_footnotes: DB: tumbil_production_db, reference date 2026-07-05, timezone America/Toronto. Placed = order_timelines.timestamp (type='placed'); delivered = payments.captured_at (status=captured, not cancelled).
Revenue methodology: all monetary values net of 13% HST (ex. HST), tips excluded.
Cost per new customer = ad spend ÷ new customers. Yesterday: $106.84 ÷ 1. 7-day rolling: $719.82 ÷ 17 = $42.34. $25 ceiling from business_context.targets.
Cancellations: Change column = day-over-day +2.1pp on the rolling 7d rate; 7d-avg column = 30-day baseline 4.8%. Failed payments: DoD count change = 0; baseline 1.2/7d.
Google Ads: account 972-828-9560, yesterday spend $106.84, last 7d pulled. Conversions count registrations, not purchases — DB used for orders.
GSC: sc-domain:tumbil.com, 2026-06-28 to 2026-07-05; indexing via URL Inspection API (42/42 indexed, no flagged pages).
Unavailable: Stripe (no API key), GA4 (Phase 2), Intercom (no API key), ClickUp (Phase 2) — no external payment cross-check today.

days.executive_summary: Solid Thursday. Delivered revenue hit $837.75 ex. HST (+32.1% vs 7d avg) on 13 orders with big baskets ($64.44 delivered AOV). Placed orders flat at 12. The sore spot: cost per new customer was $36.93 — well above the $25 ceiling — and the 7-day rolling figure ($47.46) is worse.
days.analyst_brief:
Yesterday was a good revenue day. We delivered 13 orders worth $837.75 ex. HST, about a third above the recent norm, and the orders were unusually large ($64.44 delivered average). Placed orders sat right on the 7-day line at 12. Nothing broke, ratings are still excellent (4.69, one low rating), cancellations are near zero. So the day itself is fine.

The thing worth your attention is where the ad money went. We spent $110.78 and got 3 new customers, which works out to $36.93 each — half again over the $25 ceiling. And the 7-day rolling figure is worse at $47.46, so this isn't a one-day blip; we've been paying too much to acquire customers for a week.

The leak is concentrated in one place. The **"City Target (GTA) Click" campaign spent $62.70 — more than half the entire day's ad budget — and produced zero trackable signups.** Meanwhile small campaigns did the work: Vaughan ($6.49 → 3 registrations) and Calgary ($9.59 → 1) carried the day on pennies. The GTA Click campaign is the single biggest drag on your acquisition cost. I'd take a hard look at it. Note: the registration counts come from Google's tracking, which counts signups, not real orders, so treat them as a rough guide — but a campaign spending the most and showing nothing is a clear pattern, not noise.

On the search-term side, a lot of GTA Click money is flowing to "laundromat near me" variants that aren't converting. I'm flagging this as an observation only — do **not** add negative keywords off this; that needs your manual review (past negatives blocked good searches). Just worth knowing what the budget is buying.

One quieter worry: how many new customers come back. The April groups reordered at 28–41% within three weeks, but the May groups have dropped to single digits (6.7%, 8.3% for the last two weeks shown). The most recent weeks aren't fully mature yet — the 21-day window hasn't closed — so don't over-read it, but the direction is down and it's worth watching. Paying $37–47 to acquire someone only pays off if they come back, so the acquisition-cost problem and the reorder problem compound each other.

On search rankings: Toronto (position 20.9) and Ottawa (11.8) get lots of impressions but barely any clicks because they sit too deep to be seen. The city pages share one template for titles, so this isn't a one-page fix — the body copy is the only per-city lever. Lower priority than the ad spend question.

The one thing today: figure out what the GTA Click campaign is actually doing for $60+/day, because it's the main reason new customers cost more than they should.

days.executive_summary: Saturday was a strong delivery day — 17 orders delivered for $838.12 ex. HST (+42.8% vs avg) — but acquisition was expensive: $141.90 of ad spend (the week's highest) bought only 2 new customers, a cost of $70.95 each, nearly 3x the $25 ceiling. Placed orders normal at 11.
days.analyst_brief:
**The one thing today: ad spend went up and new customers didn't.** Yesterday we spent $141.90 on Google Ads — the most in any single day this week, up about a third from the weekly average. That money brought in 2 new customers. That works out to $70.95 to land each new customer, against our ceiling of $25. Even the steadier 7-day figure is $49.85, roughly double the ceiling. This isn't a one-bad-day blip; the cost of getting a new customer has been running high all week. Spending *more* on a day that already wasn't converting well is the wrong direction.

To be clear on the math: don't be tempted to spread that $141.90 across all 11 placed orders to make it look cheaper. Eight of yesterday's eleven orders came from regulars who didn't cost us a dime in ads — they were already coming back. The ad budget's job is to find new people, and yesterday it found two of them at $70.95 apiece. That's the number that matters.

**The good news is the business underneath the ads is healthy.** Saturday delivered 17 orders worth $838.12 ex. HST — about 43% above a normal day. Regulars showed up in force (8 of 11 placed orders). Ratings stayed excellent at 4.93, with 13 of 14 reviews at five stars and no low scores. Cancellations are running 3.6% over the last week, well under the 30-day baseline of 6.6%. The three "failed payments" total 82 cents — a rounding error, not a problem. The engine runs fine; it's the fuel cost of new customers that's off.

**One thing worth watching: second-time customers were thin.** Only 1 person placed their second order yesterday, against a normal of about 2. One slow day means nothing on its own, but the rate at which new people come back for a second order has been soft for weeks — the groups of new customers from early May onward are reordering at 7–20%, down from the 30–40% we saw in April. If we're paying $50–70 to acquire someone and only a fifth of them come back, the economics get tight fast. The April iOS attribution fix helped us *see* orders better, but it didn't fix whether people return. That's the deeper lever — getting more of the expensive new customers to come back is worth more than squeezing the ad cost.

**A small SEO note, not urgent:** the blog post "How to Fold a Fitted Sheet" still isn't showing up in Google — it's been found but not indexed. Everything else (homepage, all 24 city pages, the other blogs) is indexed and healthy. If someone has 10 minutes, requesting indexing for that one page in Search Console would close the gap.

My recommendation: before adding any more ad budget, look at where yesterday's extra spend went. The GTA Click campaign took $59.78 for 2 registrations; Hamilton and Calgary each took ~$20 for zero. The cost-per-customer problem is fixable by tightening where the money goes — but that's an operator decision to review, not a knob I'd turn automatically.

days.executive_summary: Sunday placed orders dipped to 9 (vs 11.7 avg, -23.1%) and produced zero new customers — every order came from a regular. $114.61 in ad spend bought no acquisitions yesterday, and the 7-day cost per new customer sits at $62.29, well over the $25 ceiling. Deliveries were strong: 15 orders, $863.51 ex. HST (+40.2%). Ratings solid at 4.89.
days.analyst_brief:
**The one thing: we spent $114.61 yesterday and got zero new customers.** Every single one of the 9 placed orders came from a regular. That's not a one-day fluke worth worrying about on its own — Sundays are quiet — but it sits on top of a trend that is the real concern. Over the last 7 days we spent $747.54 and acquired 12 new customers. That's $62.29 to win each one, against a $25 ceiling. We are paying more than 2.5x what a new customer is supposed to cost.

The flip side is that the business is healthy *today* because regulars are carrying it. Deliveries were strong — 15 orders worth $863.51 ex. HST, up 40% on the average — and the average order size held up at $54.53. Ratings stayed high (4.89, with 8 of 9 five stars). So the operation is running well. The problem is purely at the front of the pipeline: not enough new people are coming in, and we're paying too much when they do.

It gets worse when you look at whether new customers come back. The April groups were reordering at 28–41% within three weeks. The May groups have dropped to 6.7–19%. So we're paying more to acquire people *and* a smaller share of them are returning. That combination — rising cost to acquire, falling return rate — is the thing I'd want the founders chewing on this week. It's worth asking what changed between the April and May customers: different cities, different search terms, different first-order experience? The data here can't answer it alone, but it's the highest-leverage question on the table.

One observation, not a recommendation: a chunk of yesterday's ad spend went to "dry cleaning" and "dry wash" searches (about $9 across three terms, zero orders). Tumbil doesn't do dry cleaning, so that's wasted money attracting the wrong intent. I'm flagging it for manual review only — do not touch negative keywords without operator sign-off, that's bitten us before.

On the search side, two city pages are getting heavy Google exposure but almost no clicks: Ottawa (595 impressions, 2 clicks, 0.34% click rate) and Hamilton (385 impressions, 0.52%). Both rank around position 12 — just off page one. Worth knowing the title/description that controls click-through is shared across all 24 city pages (it's a template formula in Webflow), so any rewrite affects every city, not just these two. Could be a meaningful lever given the volume, but it's a template-level decision, not a one-page fix.

Two housekeeping notes: the "how to fold a fitted sheet" blog post is still not indexed by Google ("discovered, not yet crawled") — minor, but it's the only one of 42 monitored pages that's dark. And failed payments are at 3 over the rolling week vs a 30-day baseline of under 1 — small absolute numbers ($70.57), flat day-over-day, but worth a glance to make sure it's not a pattern forming.

days.executive_summary: Quiet Monday: 10 orders placed (vs 12.1 avg, -17.4%) and only 8 delivered (vs 11.7, -31.6%), so delivered revenue dropped to $397.88 ex. HST (-35.7%). Bright spots — average order size rose to $60.63 (+13.8%) and 3 new customers showed up. Cost per new customer was $33.50, over the $25 ceiling but well below the $52.79 7-day rolling figure.
days.analyst_brief:
Monday was a slow day, but mostly in a way you'd expect after a strong weekend (Fri 17 delivered, Sat 15). Eight deliveries pulled revenue down hard on paper (-35.7%), yet the orders that did come in were bigger — average order size hit $60.63, the top of the recent range. Nothing in the day looks broken: no cancellations, ratings fine at 4.6, spend roughly flat. Don't over-read the red on delivered revenue; it's a low-count day, not a trend break.

The genuinely good news is on acquisition cost. Three new customers came in at $33.50 each. That's over the $25 ceiling for the day, but the 7-day rolling cost per new customer is $52.79 — so yesterday was actually the cheapest acquisition day in the last week by a wide margin, and three new customers beats the typical two. The trajectory here is improving, not deteriorating. I would not touch ad spend on the back of one quiet Monday.

The thing I'd actually focus on is whether new customers come back. The numbers tell a clear, uncomfortable story: of the people who first ordered in April, 28-41% placed a second order within three weeks. For the groups from May, that's collapsed to 6.7%, 19%, 18.8%, and 16.7%. We're paying real money to acquire people who increasingly don't return. Yesterday's zero second-order customers is noise on a 10-order day, but the multi-week reorder slide is signal. This is the highest-leverage problem in the whole report — it's worth more than any keyword or city page. If those May groups reordered at April's rate, we'd have roughly double the repeat volume we're seeing now. I'd spend an hour this week figuring out what changed between April and May customers: pricing, the first-order experience, the follow-up nudge, delivery timing — something shifted. Worth pulling a handful of those one-and-done May customers and looking at their orders by hand.

Two smaller things. Failed payments sit at 3 in the rolling week versus a baseline under 1 — small absolute numbers ($70.57 total), but it's been creeping, so worth a glance to make sure it's not a card-update or retry issue. And on search: Hamilton, Kitchener, and Ottawa city pages are each getting 400+ views in Google search but essentially no clicks (Hamilton 417 views → 1 click, Kitchener 479 → 1, Ottawa 453 → 1). Google is showing these pages to lots of people who don't click. Remember the title/description is a shared template across all 24 city pages, so this isn't a one-page tweak — but the high-impression, low-click pattern suggests the shared snippet isn't compelling, and that's a lever sitting in front of a lot of free traffic. Lastly, the "how to fold a fitted sheet" blog post is still sitting un-indexed (Google found it but hasn't added it) — minor, but a manual re-submit in Search Console would fix it.

days.executive_summary: Tuesday placed 10 orders (vs 11.9 avg, -16%), an unremarkable day carried by regulars (7 of 10). Delivered side was strong: $743.15 ex. HST, +11.6%, on bigger-than-usual orders. Cost per new customer was $33.69 — over the $25 ceiling, but on 3 new customers (above the 2.4 norm), and the 7-day figure is easing to $45.24.
days.analyst_brief:
Tuesday was a quiet, ordinary day on the front end — 10 orders placed, a touch below your normal — but the money that actually landed was healthy. The 12 orders you delivered brought in $743 (ex. HST), up about 12%, because the orders that closed out were larger than usual ($61.93 each delivered vs a $54.86 average). Nothing broke; this was a normal slow Tuesday, not an alarm.

The one thing worth your attention is the come-back rate, and it's been creeping up on us. Yesterday, again, **zero second-order customers** — nobody placing their second-ever order. One quiet day doesn't matter, but look at the trend: of the people who first tried us in late May, only about 1 in 6 came back within three weeks, and the early-June group is sitting at zero so far (small group, only 4 people, so not conclusive). Back in April we were getting 30–40% of new customers to return. Something changed between April and now in how well a first-time customer's experience converts into a habit. That's the lever that decides whether your ad spend compounds or leaks. I'd dig into what the April first-timers experienced that the May/June ones didn't — turnaround time, a follow-up nudge, a promo that's since lapsed. This is more valuable than anything in the ad account right now.

On acquisition cost: yesterday's spend bought 3 new customers at **$33.69 each — over your $25 ceiling**. Don't dress that up; it's above target. But two things soften it. You got 3 new customers on a day you'd normally expect 2.4, and the steadier 7-day figure ($45.24 per new customer) is actually trending down toward the ceiling, not away from it. The fix here isn't cheaper clicks — it's the come-back problem above. If those new customers came back at April's rate, $33 to acquire one is a bargain.

Two small housekeeping notes. Failed payments are at 3 in the last 7 days ($70.57) against a baseline of under 1 — still tiny numbers, but worth a 5-minute glance to make sure it's not a recurring card or a checkout glitch. And one blog post, the "how to fold a fitted sheet" guide, has been found by Google but never actually crawled or indexed — it's been sitting in limbo. If you want it earning traffic, request indexing for it in Search Console; otherwise it's dead weight.

Ratings remain excellent (4.86, no low scores yesterday), cancellations are calm and well below your 30-day norm, and your branded searches keep converting. The business is steady. The single highest-leverage question this week is: **why did our first-time customers stop coming back after April, and how do we get that back?**

days.executive_summary: Wed placed orders hit 16 (vs 12.3 avg, +30.1%) on $1,014.26 order value ex. HST (+51.1%), driven by a jump in second-order customers to 4. Cost per new customer was $30.42 — above the $25 ceiling, but the 7-day average ($41.84) is trending down. Deliveries softer at 10 ($514.51 ex. HST).
days.analyst_brief:
Good Wednesday on the order side. 16 placed orders and $1,014.26 of order value ex. HST — both well above the 7-day pace. The standout is who showed up: four customers placing their second-ever order, versus a 7-day average of about 1.6. New customers (3) and regulars (9) were both roughly normal, so the lift came from people coming back for round two. That's the part of the pipeline I most want to see healthy, because the gap between a first order and a second order is where we lose the most people.

On cost: yesterday's spend ($91.25) bought 3 new customers at $30.42 each — over our $25 ceiling. I won't dress that up by spreading the spend across all 16 orders; the 13 repeat orders didn't cost us acquisition money, so they can't lower the number. The honest read is two-part: yesterday's per-customer cost was high on low volume, but the 7-day cost per new customer is $41.84 and has been falling, so the direction of travel is right. Three new customers on a day is thin — that's the thing to watch, not panic over. If new-customer counts stay at 2–3 for a few more days while spend holds, we're paying too much for too few.

A worry worth flagging plainly: how many new customers come back is sliding. The group we got the week of April 13 had 41% order again within three weeks; every week since has been lower, and the week of June 1 (only 6 new customers) sits at 0% so far — though that week's window is still open and the small count makes it noisy. The strong second-order day yesterday is encouraging against that backdrop, but one day doesn't reverse a two-month drift. If there's one thing to dig into this week, it's why newer customers aren't coming back at April's rate.

Delivered numbers were softer — 10 orders, $514.51 ex. HST, down about 21% on revenue versus the 7-day average. Nothing alarming; delivered volume bounces day to day and yesterday's big placed haul will land over the next couple of days. Ratings were perfect (10 of 10 at five stars), cancellations and failed payments are both low and falling. No quality fires.

On search: nothing urgent. All 42 monitored pages are indexed. The Hamilton and Toronto city pages keep getting lots of Google impressions but very few clicks (Hamilton 400 impressions, 2 clicks; Toronto 320 impressions, 2 clicks) — they're ranking on page 2, so people see them but rarely click. Worth remembering the city-page title and description are a shared template across all 24 pages, so that's not a one-page tweak. The bright spot is the hotel-laundry blog post pulling 14 clicks and Niagara's city page at 6 — those are earning their keep.

The one thing: keep an eye on new-customer volume and whether they come back. Yesterday's repeat-buyer strength is the good news; the multi-week slide in reorders is the thing that decides the next quarter.

acquisition.source_note: Historical source mix is limited to TGE daily acquisition metrics. Live same-day source labels use best-effort GA4/BigQuery joins.

days.executive_summary: Thursday was a regulars day: 10 of 11 placed orders came from returning customers, only 1 new. Delivered revenue was strong at $788.76 ex. HST (+21.7%) on an unusually high delivered order size of $71.71. But with just 1 new customer against $91.17 of ad spend, cost per new customer blew past the $25 ceiling.
days.analyst_brief:
**The day was carried entirely by people who already know us.** Of 11 orders placed, 7 were regulars, 3 were second-timers, and exactly 1 was a brand-new customer. That's the cleanest "returning customer" day in the last week. The good news inside that: second orders jumped to 3 (vs ~1.7 typical), meaning a few of last month's first-timers came back. That's the habit loop working.

**But acquisition essentially stalled.** We spent $91.17 on ads and got one new customer out of it, so the cost to acquire that customer was $91.17 — nearly four times the $25 ceiling. Don't over-read a single day: on a day with one acquisition, the per-customer cost is mostly noise, swinging wildly with the count. The number that actually matters is the 7-day rolling cost per new customer, which sits at $45.84. That's the real signal, and it's been running roughly double the ceiling. Over the last 7 days, $733 of ad spend bought 16 new customers. The ads are still finding people — yesterday just happened to land only one. I would not touch budgets off one slow day, but if the rolling number stays north of $40 into next week, the spend efficiency is the thing to dig into, not the daily wobble.

**Delivered revenue looked great, but understand why.** We delivered $788.76 ex. HST, up 22%, on a delivered order size of $71.71 — well above the usual ~$57. That jump is about a few large orders being delivered yesterday, not a price change or a sudden surge in demand. It's a timing-and-size effect from regulars sending bigger loads. Real and bankable, but don't extrapolate it into a trend.

**One thing to keep an eye on: cancellations.** The rolling 7-day cancel rate is 5.9%, up 2.4 points from where the same metric sat the day before. Two orders cancelled yesterday, five in the last week against just one the week prior. Absolute numbers are tiny, so this could easily be random — but the direction is worth a glance. If you have a quick way to see why those two cancelled (customer change of mind vs. a supply/scheduling problem), it's worth 5 minutes. The 30-day baseline is 5.8%, so we're right at normal, not above it yet.

**Ratings are fine.** 4.75 yesterday across 8 ratings, no low scores, 6 of 8 were five stars. The one 3-star this week was back on the 22nd. Nothing to act on.

**On the marketing site:** all 42 monitored pages are indexed by Google, nothing flagged. The hotel-laundry blog post keeps pulling its weight — 733 impressions, 13 clicks. Hamilton's city page is the one underperformer worth noting: it got 418 times in front of searchers but only 3 clicks (0.72%), sitting at position ~14, just off page 1. Because the city pages all share one template for the title and description, that's not a Hamilton-specific copy fix — it's a question of whether the shared template title could be sharpened for everyone. Worth a look when there's time, not urgent.

**The one thing:** nothing today demands action. Watch the rolling cost-per-new-customer and the cancel uptick over the next few days; both are early flickers, not fires.

acquisition.source_note: Historical source mix is limited to TGE daily acquisition metrics. Live same-day source labels use best-effort GA4/BigQuery joins.

days.executive_summary: Friday was a strong volume day — 16 placed orders (+34.5% vs 11.9 avg) worth $733.27 ex. HST. But it ran almost entirely on regulars (13 of 16); just 1 new customer came in against $100.46 of ad spend, so yesterday's cost per new customer was $100.46. Deliveries were soft at 10 ($513.38 ex. HST, -22.9%).
days.analyst_brief:
Yesterday was a good day on the surface and a thin one underneath. We placed 16 orders — well above the 11.9 daily average — but 13 of them came from regulars, our highest regular share in the week (81%). Only one brand-new customer showed up, and second-time customers were two. So the volume is real, but it's our existing base reordering, not new demand arriving.

That's the thing to sit with. We spent $100.46 on ads and got one new customer out of it, which makes yesterday's cost per new customer $100.46 — four times the $25 ceiling. I want to be careful here: that's a single-day, one-customer number, and one-customer days swing wildly. The steadier read is the 7-day rolling cost per new customer, which is $57.10. That's still more than double the ceiling, and it's been creeping the wrong way. Don't over-react to the $100 figure, but do treat the $57 as the honest signal: we are currently paying too much for each new customer.

The deeper worry is what happens after we acquire them. The reorder data is sliding. The group of new customers we got the week of June 1 — 11 people — have so far produced zero repeat orders inside the 21-day window. The weeks before that (May 18, May 25) are sitting at 19% and 17%. Compare that to April, when we were doing 30-41%. New customers are getting more expensive to acquire AND fewer of them are coming back. Those two trends together are the real story this week, more than any single day's order count. If we're paying $57 to acquire someone and a smaller fraction returns, the math on paid acquisition gets worse every week we don't address it. I'd put founder attention on the first-order experience and a deliberate nudge to get people to order a second time — that lever matters more right now than anything in the ad account.

A few smaller notes. Average order size on placed orders dipped to $45.83 (vs $53.74), but that's just because regulars tend to place smaller, more frequent orders and they dominated yesterday — it's not anyone reacting to price. Cancellations are fine and actually improving: the rolling 7-day rate fell to 4.8%, down 2.3 points from the prior reading, and below the 30-day baseline of 6.0%. Failed payments are quiet at 2. Ratings are excellent — 4.93 yesterday on 14 ratings, 13 of them five stars, no low scores.

On search: nothing on fire. All 42 monitored pages are indexed. One pattern worth a future look — Hamilton, Mississauga, and Kitchener city pages get a lot of impressions on Google (419, 306, 356) but barely any clicks (under 1% click-through). Since the page titles are a shared template across all 24 city pages, that's a template-level rewrite to test, not a one-city fix — park it for a low-traffic day, it's not today's priority. The reorder problem is.

days.executive_summary: Saturday placed orders were light at 10 vs 11.7 avg (-14.5%), while deliveries were strong at 15 (+29.3%). Spend was normal at $103.35, but only 2 new customers means cost per new customer was $51.67, still far above the $25 ceiling.
days.analyst_brief:
The one thing I would pay attention to today is still acquisition cost. Saturday spend was basically normal at $103.35, but it produced only 2 new customers, so each new customer cost $51.67. That is better than the 7-day average of $58.65, but both are more than 2x the $25 ceiling. This is not a volume problem alone: yesterday had 10 placed orders and 15 delivered orders, so the service is moving work through the system. The growth issue is that paid spend is not buying enough first-time customers.

The customer mix explains the day. Of 10 placed orders, 7 came from regulars, 1 came from a second-order customer, and only 2 were first orders. That is a decent business-quality signal because the product is still being used by people who already know it. It is not enough to grow daily order count toward the 20-order breakeven target. Regulars are keeping the floor under revenue; new customers are not lifting the ceiling.

Quality was clean: 11 ratings, all 5-star, with no low-rating alerts. Failed payments improved too, with the 7-day count falling from 2 to 1 versus yesterday's rolling reading. The one operational watchout is cancellations: the 7-day cancellation rate moved up from 4.8% to 6.1% day over day, now slightly above the 30-day baseline of 5.5%. That is not a crisis, but it is the only service-health metric moving the wrong way.

Search has a more actionable signal than yesterday's paid search terms. The city pages are fully indexed, so this is not an indexing problem. Hamilton is getting seen a lot but barely clicked: 440 impressions, 3 clicks, 0.68% click-through rate, average position 14. Mississauga is similar: 304 impressions, 2 clicks, 0.66% click-through rate, average position 8.7. Kitchener is even more extreme: 358 impressions, 1 click, 0.28% click-through rate, average position 9.0. Because city page meta titles and descriptions are template-level across all 24 city pages, the practical move is not a one-page Hamilton tweak; it is to review whether the shared city-page title/description is failing to earn clicks for pages already visible in Google.

Highest-leverage next move: spend 30 minutes on the city-page search result copy, not ad negatives. The paid account is spending steadily but buying too few new customers; organic city pages already have impressions and indexing. Improving clicks where Tumbil is already appearing is the smallest change with a plausible path to cheaper new orders.

days.executive_summary: Sunday placed orders fell to 9 vs 11.7 avg (-23.1%), with placed order value down to $379.50 ex. HST (-39.3%). The real problem was acquisition: $121.26 in ad spend produced 1 new customer, so cost per new customer was $121.26 vs the $25 ceiling.
days.analyst_brief:
Sunday was not a demand win. Orders placed were 9, below the 11.7 daily average, and the average placed order size was only $42.17 ex. HST, below the $45 floor. That is why placed order value dropped harder than order count: $379.50 ex. HST vs the $625.59 average. Deliveries looked fine at 11 orders and $657.13 ex. HST, but that is yesterday's fulfillment/finance result, not proof that Sunday acquisition worked.

The main issue is paid growth efficiency. Google Ads spent $121.26 and the DB shows only 1 new customer. That means cost per new customer was $121.26, far above the $25 ceiling. The 7-day average is also poor at $54.65, so this is not just one noisy Sunday. Do not soften this by spreading spend across all 9 placed orders; 6 of those orders came from regulars and 2 from second-order customers. They did not consume yesterday's acquisition budget. The right read is simple: yesterday's spend bought 1 new customer at a price Tumbil cannot scale.

The customer mix also tells the same story. Only 1 new customer showed up vs a 1.9 average, while 2 second-order customers came back vs a 1.4 average. That is good for retention, but it does not solve the front problem. Regulars still made up most of the day with 6 orders, but they were also below the 8.4 average. So Sunday was weaker on both new demand and repeat demand, with the biggest pain in new-customer cost.

The strongest near-term organic signal is still city pages. All 42 monitored pages are indexed, so Google can see the work. The issue is getting more clicks from pages already showing up. Hamilton had 455 impressions but only 3 clicks at position 13.5. Kitchener had 365 impressions and only 1 click at position 9.0. Mississauga had 285 impressions and 2 clicks at position 8.8. Those are not broken pages; they are pages Google is testing but searchers are mostly passing over. Because city page meta titles/descriptions are template-level across all 24 city pages, treat this as a city-template problem, not a one-page tweak.

One practical move today: audit the city page search snippets and body intro against the high-impression, low-click pages, especially Hamilton, Kitchener, Mississauga, Toronto, and St. Catharines. The goal is not a big SEO project; it is making the search result and first few lines more obviously about pickup and delivery laundry in that city at $1.25/lb. That is more defensible than pushing more paid spend while the 7-day cost per new customer is $54.65.

days.executive_summary: Monday was a blowout for orders coming in: 25 placed (vs 13.9 avg, +79.9%) worth $1,333.77 ex. HST (+82.8%). New customers tripled to 5 at $21.22 each — under the $25 ceiling. Deliveries lagged at 11 ($468.25 ex. HST, -25.2%), which is just timing — those 25 placed orders deliver over the next few days.
days.analyst_brief:
Yesterday was the best order-intake day in at least two weeks. 25 orders came in versus the usual ~14, worth $1,334 ex. HST. This wasn't one whale skewing things — the average order size was dead normal at $53.35. It was simply more orders across the board: 17 regulars, 5 brand-new customers, and 3 second-timers. Every customer group came in well above its typical day.

The acquisition side is the standout. We picked up 5 new customers at $21.22 each, comfortably under the $25 ceiling. That's the first day in a while where new-customer cost looked healthy — the 7-day rolling figure is still elevated at $47.74, dragged up by thin acquisition days earlier in the month, so don't celebrate the rolling number yet. But a 5-new-customer day at $21 each is exactly the shape we want, and ad spend ($106) was right on its normal level, so this wasn't bought with extra budget.

Don't be thrown by the delivered numbers looking soft (11 delivered, $468 revenue, -25%). Placed and delivered are on different clocks — orders placed yesterday get washed and delivered over the following days. The 25 we took in will land in the delivered/revenue figures across this week. If anything, expect a strong revenue day or two coming up. The low delivered average order size yesterday ($42.57) is just which orders happened to finish that day, not a pricing problem.

One thing to keep an eye on: cancellations. The rolling 7-day cancel rate is 7.2%, up 1.1 points from the day before and above our 30-day baseline of 4.8%. In absolute terms it's 7 cancels out of 97 placed — not a crisis, and a couple of those came on the big-volume day. But it's been creeping the wrong way, so if it's still climbing in a few days it's worth understanding why people are cancelling. Failed payments, by contrast, are clean: zero in the last 7 days.

On the website/search side, nothing's broken — all 42 monitored pages are indexed. The one recurring opportunity is Hamilton: its city page got 450 views in search but only a 0.67% click rate at position 13.2 (just below page 1). Kitchener (384 impressions, 0.26% clicks) and Mississauga (290 impressions, 0.34%) have the same problem — lots of people seeing us, almost nobody clicking. The catch: the page title and description are a shared template across all 24 city pages, so you can't tweak Hamilton's headline alone. If you want to lift click rates here, it's a template-level title rewrite that moves all 24 at once — a 20-minute experiment worth running, but treat it as a batch, not a one-city fix.

Bottom line: strong day, healthy acquisition cost, watch the cancel creep.

days.executive_summary: Tuesday placed 13 orders (vs 14.3 avg, -9.1%) but delivered a strong 16 ($898.88 ex. HST, +38.7%). Average order size jumped to $73.85. Only 1 new customer showed up, so cost per new customer hit $79.62 — more than triple the $25 ceiling. Two 1-star ratings landed the same day.
days.analyst_brief:
**The money side of yesterday was good; the growth side was not.**

Delivered revenue came in at $898.88 ex. HST on 16 orders — the best day of the week and 39% above the 7-day average. Placed order value was also strong at $960.03, with the average order size climbing to $73.85. But be clear-eyed about *why* the average is high: yesterday was carried almost entirely by people who already know us. Of 13 placed orders, 8 were regulars and 4 were second-timers — 12 of 13 repeat business. The bigger orders came from that loyal base, not from new demand. This is a great sign for retention and a warning sign for growth.

**Only one new customer walked in the door.** We spent $79.62 on ads to get that one person, so yesterday's cost to acquire a new customer was $79.62 — more than three times our $25 ceiling. Don't soften that by spreading the ad spend across all 13 orders; the 12 repeat customers weren't bought with yesterday's ad budget, so they can't be used to make the acquisition math look better. The fairer read is the 7-day rolling figure: $49.61 per new customer, still roughly double the ceiling. Google Ads also only logged 2 sign-ups on 66 clicks and 805 impressions — the front of the order pipeline is genuinely thin right now, and it has been trending that way (new customers averaged just 2 per day this week).

**The reorder picture underneath this is the thing I'd worry about most.** The customers we picked up in late May and early June are barely coming back — the June 1 week shows 0 of 14 returning within three weeks, and the two weeks before it were in the teens. Some of that is the 21-day window not being fully elapsed yet, so don't panic on the newest weeks, but the direction has been soft since early May. Acquiring expensive customers who don't reorder is the worst combination. If you're going to spend time this week, spend it on why the last two months of new customers aren't coming back, not on ad tweaks.

**One operational flag:** two 1-star ratings came in yesterday (orders 6406 and 6468, from two different WashPros — 3419 and 4788). That's what pulled the daily rating down to 4.47 from an otherwise near-perfect week. Two independent bad experiences on the same day is worth a quick look — check whether either is a service issue we can fix before it shows up in reorders.

Everything else is quiet: all 42 monitored pages are indexed, cancellations ticked up only 0.8 points day-over-day (though the 7-day rate at 9% is running above the 5.3% monthly baseline — keep half an eye on it), and failed payments are negligible at $6.27 total. Hamilton's city page is worth a note: it got 434 views but almost no clicks (0.46%) — but remember the city page titles are one shared template across all 24 cities, so that's a template-level rewrite to evaluate, not a one-page fix.

days.executive_summary: Wednesday placed orders collapsed to 4 (vs 12.6 avg, -68.3%) — all four were regulars, with zero new and zero second-order customers. Ad spend of $88 bought no new customers, so the day's cost per new customer is undefined; the 7-day rolling figure sits at $62.85, 2.5x the $25 ceiling. Deliveries held at 8 ($410.50 ex. HST).
days.analyst_brief:
Wednesday was a quiet order day — 4 placed — but the number that actually matters is who those orders came from: all four were regulars. Zero new customers, zero second-timers. So the low headline is only half the story. The real signal is that the front of the pipeline went silent.

Don't over-read the -68% vs the 7-day average. That average is inflated by a freak 25-order Monday (6/29) and a strong 6/30. A 4-order Wednesday is a bad day, not a broken system — deliveries still ran at 8 and the app is fulfilling fine. One quiet day, on its own, I'd shrug at.

What I wouldn't shrug at is acquisition. Yesterday's $88 in ad spend produced no new customers, so there's no cost-per-new-customer to report for the day — the spend simply didn't buy anyone. Zoom out and it's not a one-day fluke: over the last 7 days we spent ~$691 and got 11 new customers, which works out to **$62.85 to acquire each one — about 2.5x the $25 ceiling we hold ourselves to.** We are paying up for growth we're barely getting. That is the leak, and it's been open for more than a day.

It gets worse when you look at whether those expensive new customers come back. The groups we picked up in late May and early June are reordering weakly — the group from the week of June 1 had *zero* of its 14 new customers order again within three weeks. So we're overpaying to acquire people who then don't stick. Fixing either half (cheaper acquisition or better second-order conversion) is worth more than any single day's order count.

A couple of smaller things worth your eye:

- **Cancellations.** The 7-day cancellation rate is 9.1%, up against a 30-day baseline of 5.5%. Day-over-day it's flat (+0.1pp), so nothing broke yesterday — but the elevated 7-day level is being driven by clusters on 6/25 and 6/29 (3 cancels each). Keep half an eye on it; if it stays above ~8% next week it's a pattern, not noise.
- **Ratings are healthy** (4.71 yesterday, 4.82 over 7 days). Two 1-star ratings landed on 6/30 (washpros 3419 and 4788) — no repeat offenders flagged, so likely one-offs, but worth a glance if you have a minute.

On the search side, all 42 monitored pages are indexed — no problems there. One recurring observation: the Hamilton and Kitchener city pages get a lot of Google impressions (421 and 346) but almost nobody clicks (0.24% and 0.29%). That usually means the title/description isn't compelling. Worth knowing that the meta title and description are a single template shared across all 24 city pages, so this isn't a one-page tweak — it's one change that would move all of them. Might be worth 20 minutes with whoever owns Webflow.

The one thing: acquisition cost, not yesterday's order count. We're paying 2.5x target for new customers who aren't reordering. That's where the leverage is.

days.executive_summary: Thursday placed orders fell to 8 (vs 12.1 avg, -33.9%), but the money side held up: delivered revenue was $802.02 ex. HST (+26.3%) on 12 deliveries, and placed orders were unusually large at $76.44 avg (+35.5%). 3 new customers came in at $39.62 each — above the $25 ceiling but well under the $55 7-day trend.
days.analyst_brief:
Two different stories today, so I'll keep them apart.

**The order count looked bad; the money didn't.** Only 8 orders were placed yesterday — a third below the usual ~12, and the second thin day in a row after Wednesday's 4. But don't read that as a revenue problem. On the delivery side (the number that actually bills), we did 12 orders and $802 ex. HST, which is +26% over trend. And the orders that did come in were big: $76.44 average, our highest placed order size in the 7-day window. So the day was quiet on volume but healthy on dollars.

**Why were the orders so large?** It's not a price change — it's who ordered. 5 of the 8 were regulars and 3 were brand-new, with zero second-timers. Regulars tend to place bigger, more predictable orders, and new customers came in heavier than usual too. Nothing to fix here; just know the high average order size is composition, not a pricing signal, and it'll drift back down on a normal-mix day.

**New-customer cost is the thing to watch.** We spent $118.87 (up 16% over trend) and got 3 new customers, so each one cost $39.62. That's above our $25 ceiling — but it's the best single-day acquisition cost we've had in a while: the 7-day rolling cost per new customer is $55, so yesterday actually pulled the trend in the right direction. I wouldn't panic on one day. What I'd flag is *where* the spend went: the "City Target (GTA) Click" campaign burned $50.94 — nearly half the day's budget — for 39 clicks and zero registrations. Ottawa ($9.39 → 2) and the smaller city campaigns did the real work. If GTA Click keeps posting a goose-egg while eating the biggest slice, it's worth a manual look at that campaign, not a keyword change.

**The soft spot underneath all this is repeat.** Second-order customers were zero yesterday, and the "how many came back within 3 weeks" numbers have been weak — the late-May and early-June groups reordered at 0–19%, well off the ~30% we saw in April. We're paying up to acquire people who increasingly aren't coming back for a second order. That's the real leak, and it matters more than any single low-volume day.

**Everything else is fine.** Ratings steady at 4.73 (one 3-star, no crisis). Cancellations are actually falling — the rolling rate ticked down 2 points day-over-day to 7.1%, still a hair above the 5.8% 30-day baseline but trending the right way. One tiny failed payment ($0.31), no issue. All 42 monitored pages are indexed. Hamilton's city page keeps getting seen (419 impressions) with zero clicks at position 12 — same story as prior days; a title/body refresh there is a low-effort SEO nibble whenever there's a spare 20 minutes, but it's not today's priority. Today's priority is the repeat problem.

days.executive_summary: Friday placed orders jumped to 17 (vs 12.3 avg, +38.2%), the best day in a week — 12 regulars plus 4 new customers. Cost per new customer was $29.16, over the $25 ceiling but far below the 7-day average of $45.86. Deliveries were light at 8 ($451.27 ex. HST), a normal pipeline-lag day.
days.analyst_brief:
**Friday was your strongest ordering day in a week, and the good part is it wasn't luck.** 17 orders placed, up from a 12.3 average. The engine was your regulars — 12 of the 17 came from customers who've ordered 3+ times before. But you also pulled in 4 brand-new customers, nearly double the ~2/day you normally see. So this wasn't a "regulars carried a slow day" story; both the loyal base and new demand showed up at the same time.

**On acquisition cost: you spent $116.63 on ads and got 4 new customers, which works out to $29.16 each.** That's over your $25 ceiling, so it's not a clean pass. But it's the cheapest new-customer day in a while — your 7-day rolling cost is $45.86, so yesterday was 36% below that trend. The direction is good. I would not read $29.16 as a problem; I'd read it as the first day in a while where paid spend actually produced customers efficiently. Keep an eye on whether it holds for a couple more days before calling it a real improvement.

**The soft-looking number — 8 deliveries, revenue $451.27 ex. HST, down ~28% from average — is a timing artifact, not a demand problem.** Deliveries lag placements by a day or two, and your placed side is clearly healthy. Nothing to act on there; it'll catch up.

**A couple of small watch items, none urgent:**

- Only 1 second-order customer yesterday, below the ~1.4 norm. On its own that's noise. But the longer reorder picture is worth watching: of the new customers you got in early June, very few came back within three weeks (the June 1 week group had 0 of 14 return, June 8 had 2 of 9). That's the metric I'd care about more than any single day — you're getting new customers in the door, but the come-back rate has been weak since May. If you want one thing to think about this week, it's what happens to a new customer in their first two weeks, not today's ad cost.

- Ratings are steady at 4.78 with one 3-star yesterday. Fine. No pattern in the low ratings across the week, no WashPro flagged.

**On search:** nothing broken. All 42 monitored pages are indexed. Your Hamilton city page keeps getting shown by Google (408 impressions in the last 7 days) but got zero clicks and sits around position 12 — it's being seen but not ranking high enough to earn clicks. Kitchener is similar (423 impressions, 1 click). Since the city pages all share one meta title/description template, a rewrite there would move all 24 at once — that's a real lever if you want an SEO project, but it's not today's fire.

**Bottom line:** healthy demand day, acquisition cost trending the right way, and the one thing genuinely worth your attention is the weak come-back rate on recent new customers — not anything that happened yesterday.

days.executive_summary: Saturday was a quiet holiday-weekend day: 7 orders placed (vs 11.9 avg, -41.2%) and only 6 delivered for $277.13 ex. HST — half the usual daily revenue (-51.1%). Average order size placed jumped to $83.39 (+39.6%), and cost to acquire a new customer was $34.59, above the $25 ceiling but below the 7-day trend.
days.analyst_brief:
Nothing broke yesterday — it was just a slow Saturday on a holiday weekend. Seven orders in, six delivered. Revenue of $277 ex. HST landed at half the usual day, which tripped the anomaly flag, but that's volume, not a problem. Ratings were a clean 5.0 across three responses, cancellations and failed payments are quiet. Don't read too much into a single low-volume weekend day.

Two things are actually worth your attention.

**The customer mix flipped, and it explains the odd numbers.** Normally two-thirds of a day's orders come from regulars. Yesterday only 2 of 7 did — regulars dropped 74% below their usual level. Instead the day was carried by newcomers: 3 brand-new customers and 2 second-timers made up 5 of 7 orders. Here's the interesting part: even with regulars mostly absent, the average order placed was $83, way above the usual $60. So the big baskets yesterday came from the new and second-time customers, not the regulars. That's a good sign for those newcomers — people placing a big first or second order tend to stick. Worth watching whether they come back.

**The persistent cost to acquire a new customer is the real story — not yesterday specifically.** Yesterday's $34.59 per new customer is over the $25 ceiling, but it was actually *better* than where we've been: the 7-day rolling cost sits at $43.19, and it's been stuck above the ceiling for a while. That's the number to fix. To be clear on the math — we spent $103.76 and got 3 new customers, so $34.59 each; I'm not spreading that spend across all 7 orders, because the regulars who ordered yesterday didn't cost us any ad money. The question isn't "was Saturday expensive," it's "why has each new customer been costing us ~$43 for a week?" That's where I'd point engineering/marketing time this week — the ad money is flowing (spend is dead steady at ~$105/day) but the conversion from click to paying newcomer is soft.

One small observation, no action needed: a few city pages are getting seen a lot on Google but nobody clicks. Hamilton had 409 views and zero clicks (ranking ~12th, so it's on page 2 — that's why). Kitchener got 435 views and one click. Toronto sits at position 21. These aren't quick fixes — the page titles and descriptions are set by one shared template across all 24 city pages, so you can't rewrite just one. Flagging it as a pattern, not a to-do.

Everything is indexed and healthy on the search side — all 42 monitored pages are live in Google.

days.executive_summary: Sunday deliveries were strong — 13 orders worth $916.76 ex. HST (+51.9% vs 7d avg). Placed orders held steady at 12. But only 1 new customer showed up, so the day's ad spend ($106.84) bought just one acquisition, and the 7-day cost per new customer sits at $42.34 — well over the $25 ceiling.
days.analyst_brief:
Yesterday was a good financial day carried almost entirely by people who already know us. We delivered 13 orders worth $916.76 ex. HST — over half again the 7-day average — and 9 of the 12 placed orders (75%) came from regulars. Second-order and repeat customers held up nicely. The order size dipped a bit ($54.40 vs $60.82 average), but that's normal weekend variance and still comfortably above the $45 target. Ratings were excellent (4.86, no low scores). If you only looked at revenue, this was a strong Sunday.

The problem is at the front of the pipeline. Only **1 new customer** showed up. We spent $106.84 on ads to get that one person, so yesterday's cost per new customer was $106.84 — more than four times the $25 ceiling. One quiet day like that doesn't mean much on its own, which is why the honest number to watch is the 7-day rolling figure: **$42.34 per new customer** (17 new customers for $719.82 over the week). That has been sitting above $25 for a while now. Spend is steady (~$103/day) but it's converting fewer strangers into first orders. This is the one thing I'd keep my eye on — the business is running on its regulars, and the tank of new customers we refill each week is getting more expensive.

Two smaller things worth noting.

Cancellations are creeping up. The rolling 7-day cancel rate is 9.3%, up 2.1 points from the prior day's reading, and nearly double the steadier 30-day baseline of 4.8% (8 cancels this week vs 17 across the whole month). Not an emergency, but if it keeps climbing it's eating into orders we already paid to acquire. Worth a glance at what those 8 cancelled orders had in common.

Ad money is leaking into generic searches. A chunk of yesterday's spend went to broad "laundromat near me" / "laundry near me" clicks that produced zero orders (roughly $20+ across those terms). I'm flagging this as an observation only — do not add negative keywords without reviewing manually, since past broad negatives blocked good searches. Just something for a human to eyeball.

On SEO: all 42 monitored pages are indexed, nothing broken. The Hamilton city page got 399 impressions and **zero clicks** (ranking ~12.6, just off page 1), and Toronto is stuck at position 21.6 despite 417 impressions. Google is showing these pages but nobody's clicking. Since the city pages share one template for their title and description, a rewrite there would lift all 24 at once — a higher-leverage 20-minute job than tweaking a single page.
